nativeRange.habitat: Reef edges and slopes over sand and rubble in the Indian Ocean, typically encountered in small groups.

decorPreferences: fine to medium sand bed for nightly burrowing
live rock with open swimming space
tightly sealed lid to prevent jumping

diet.notes: Generally accepts a variety of meaty foods including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and high-quality marine pellets. Has been reported to pick at and consume nuisance flatworms and pyramid snails, which some keepers value as pest control.

goodWith: most peaceful to semi-aggressive community fish
avoidWith: large predatory fish that may eat them

summary: The Canary Wrasse (Halichoeres leucoxanthus) is a small, bright-yellow Indian Ocean wrasse generally considered reef-safe with caution. It needs a fine sand bed for nightly burrowing, a tightly sealed lid, and is best kept one per tank.
careNotes: The Canary Wrasse is a small Indian Ocean species, reaching around 10 to 12 cm as an adult, that is closely related to but distinct from the Yellow Coris Wrasse (Halichoeres chrysus). The two species look superficially similar: the Canary Wrasse has a white underside and yellow restricted to the upper body, while the Yellow Coris Wrasse is uniformly bright yellow. Like other members of the genus Halichoeres, this species is a sand-sleeper — it dives into the substrate at night and when startled — so a fine-to-medium sand bed of at least 5 cm is essential. Bare-bottom tanks and crushed coral substrates are unsuitable. A tightly sealed lid is strongly recommended, as wrasses in this genus are known to jump from open aquariums. The Canary Wrasse is generally considered reef-safe, though it has been reported to occasionally pick at small ornamental shrimp, small hermit crabs, tubeworms, and feather dusters; reef-safe-with-caution is the conservative approach for mixed systems. It is best kept one per tank unless the aquarium is very large, as conspecifics and similarly patterned Halichoeres species tend to be harassed. A minimum tank volume of around 300 L is a reasonable guideline for a single adult with adequate swimming room. Most wrasses in this genus are protogynous hermaphrodites (meaning females can change sex to become males), and this species is thought to follow the same pattern.
breedingNotes: Not commonly bred in home aquaria.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and biological source after the designated LiveAquaria product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/canary-wrasse) returned HTTP 404.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 cm), temperament, reef-safety, diet. Recommended minimum tank 500 L — at the high end of the source spread.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com product page
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (24-28 C, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025), lifespan (5-7 years), and recommended minimum tank 35 gal (~130 L) — at the low end of the source spread. Tank-size spread across sources (130-500 L) was averaged toward the cautious-larger 300 L round number.
